dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for breathing problems.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Uh, difficulty breathing for about a week and a cough with yellow sputum.
Ma'am, are you able to open your eyes for me?
Can you tell me your name and date of birth for our records?
Our names are Alex and Jordan. Can you tell us the name of this town?
Please point to your right hand for us now.
Ma'am, can we do a quick check of your breathing? We'd like to assess how you're breathing and your airway.
Respiratory rate is 25 per minute, oxygen saturation is 99 percent, and we're using a capnography to assess your breath.
There is wheezing and crackles in both lungs, which are known as adventitious lung sounds.
Your pulse is 89, blood pressure is 121 over 84. Radial pulses are strong and equal. Skin is warm and dry.
Chest rise and fall is normal. You seem to be breathing better when you're upright.
Can I ask about your medical history?
I have a history of asthma, bronchitis, emphysema, and a heart attack last year.
Any medications you're currently taking?
I take Toprol, Microzide, aspirin, Protonix, and Zoloft.
Do you have any allergies?
No known allergies.
Have you had any chest pain, nausea, vomiting, or abdominal pain recently?
No chest pain, no nausea, vomiting, or abdominal pain.
Are you experiencing any pain at the moment?
I don't have any pain-just trouble breathing.
Head is normocephalic, eyes are equal and round at 4 mm, PERRL. No discharge from ears, nose, or throat observed.
You are showing some increased effort with accessory muscles. Breath sounds are diminished at the base with wheezing and rales on both sides.
No tenderness or distension found in the abdomen. No changes in bowel habits or recent diarrhea.
Capillary refill is less than two seconds. No swelling, deformities, or pain in the arms or legs.
You are calm and cooperative. GCS is 15, and you are alert and oriented to person, place, and time.
Pulse is 89 beats per minute.
Respiratory rate is 25 breaths per minute.
Blood pressure is 121 over 84.
Oxygen saturation is 99 percent.
Glucose is not available on this device.
EKG shows a normal sinus rhythm.
Capnography reading is normal with mild sloping, which matches lab results.
I need to apply a 4-lead ECG for continuous cardiac monitoring.
I'm preparing to take a 12-lead ECG for detailed cardiac assessment. I'll explain each step along the way.
We'll administer a nebulizer with albuterol and ipratropium. This will open your airways and help ease your breathing.
We're also going to give you dexamethasone through your IV to reduce lung inflammation.
We'll need to start an IV in your left hand to give the dexamethasone. Is that okay with you?
Successfully started an IV in your left hand without any complications.
I just reassessed. Pulse is 77, respirations 26, BP 125 over 85, and oxygen saturation is 94 percent.
Your breathing remains stable, with some improvement noted in wheezing on the left.
We're going to do another nebulizer treatment now to see if breathing improves further.
Okay, whatever helps.
We'll be taking you to Chippenham Hospital Cold for further treatment. Agree?
Yes.
We'll place you on the stretcher in a Fowler's position for comfort and breathing support. Ready?
Shoulder and lap belts will secure you during transport. All set to move.
Pre-arrival report has been sent to the hospital. ED room 23 is prepared to receive you.
We've left the apartment and everything is stable. No changes noted at this time.
Arrived at the hospital, and you were safely transferred to ED room 23.
Documentation confirms a stable patient, with no adverse changes during transport.
During transport, we continued to monitor and support your breathing and IV therapy.
No jugular venous distension noted, and Fowler's position has been maintained during transport.
Your breathing remained stable, with no signs of worsening or change in condition.
12-lead EKG still shows a normal sinus rhythm, and no new findings.
No falls, no signs of COPD exacerbation, and no signs of infection or fever or chills.
No recent exposure suspected or confirmed for individuals with possible or confirmed COVID-19.
All interventions have been documented in the EPCR without hallucinations or fabricated information.
